467	\begin{lemma}\leanok
468	[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
469	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
470	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
471	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
472	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
473	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
474	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
475	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
476	  \[
477	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
478	  \]
479	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
480	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
481	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
482	  \[
483	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
484	  \]
485	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
486	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
487	  \[
488	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
489	      \longrightarrow 0
490	  \]
491	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
492	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
493	\end{lemma}
494	
495	\begin{proof}
496	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
497	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
498	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
499	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
500	  \leanok
501	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
502	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8), assembled from the sub-lemmas of \S``The horseshoe
503	  construction, step by step''. Take \(I_A, I_C\) and set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\):
504	  injective by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}, degreewise split by
505	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}. The twist \(\tau\) and augmentation exist with
506	  the cocycle identity (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, via
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}); the matrix differential then squares to zero
508	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}) with coprojection and projection chain maps
509	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}); and \(I_B^\bullet\) resolves \(B\)
510	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}). These data are exactly the asserted
511	  degreewise-split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A \to I_B \to I_C \to 0\) of cochain
512	  complexes compatible with the augmentations.
513	\end{proof}

515	\section{The dimension-shift step}
516	
517	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
518	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
519	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
520	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
521	complex-level homology long exact sequence
522	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
523	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
524	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
525	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
526	to objects.
527	
528	\begin{lemma}[Resolution-level dimension shift]
529	  \label{lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
530	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES, CategoryTheory.shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic}
531	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
532	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
533	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
534	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
535	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
536	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
537	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
538	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
539	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
540	  \[
541	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
542	  \]
543	\end{lemma}
544	
545	\begin{proof}
546	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
547	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
548	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
549	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
550	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
551	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
552	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
553	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
554	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
555	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
556	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
557	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
558	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
559	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).
560	\end{proof}
561	
562	\begin{lemma}\leanok
563	[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
564	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
565	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
566	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
567	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
568	    lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
569	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
570	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
571	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
573	  % between abelian categories and assume
574	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
575	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
576	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
577	  % \begin{enumerate}
578	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
579	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
580	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
581	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
582	  % \end{enumerate}
583	  % In each of the three cases
584	  % $$
585	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
586	  % $$
587	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
588	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
589	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
590	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
591	  \[
592	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
593	  \]
594	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
595	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of
596	  right-derived functors furnish isomorphisms
597	  \[
598	    (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
599	    \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
600	  \]
601	  (The companion lowest-degree statement
602	  \((R^{1} G)(A) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J) \to G(Z))\) is isolated as the
603	  sibling Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
604	\end{lemma}
605	
606	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
607	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
608	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
609	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
610	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
611	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
612	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
613	\begin{proof}
614	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
615	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
616	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
617	    \leanok
618	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
619	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
620	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
621	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
622	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
623	
624	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
625	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
626	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
627	  complexes
628	  \[
629	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
630	  \]
631	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
632	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
633	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
634	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
635	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
636	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
637	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
638	  \[
639	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
640	      \longrightarrow 0
641	  \]
642	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
643	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
644	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
645	  \[
646	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
647	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
648	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
649	  \]
650	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
651	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
652	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
653	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
654	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
655	  sequence of right-derived functors
656	  \[
657	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
658	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
659	  \]
660	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
661	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
662	  \[
663	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
664	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
665	  \]
666	
667	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
668	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
669	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
670	  \[
671	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
672	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
673	  \]
674	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
675	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism, which is the
676	  asserted dimension shift. (The lowest-degree segment of the same long exact
677	  sequence yields the cokernel description of \((R^1 G)(A)\); that reading is
678	  recorded separately in Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
679	\end{proof}

681	\begin{lemma}\leanok
682	[Lowest-degree cokernel description across an acyclic short exact sequence]
683	  \label{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}
684	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic}
685	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
686	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
687	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
688	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
689	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
690	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
691	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
692	  % between abelian categories and assume
693	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
694	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
695	  % of $\mathcal{A}$."
696	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "As $RF$ is an exact functor and since
697	  % $R^iF = 0$ for $i < 0$ and $R^0F = F$
698	  % (Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived})
699	  % we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
700	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
701	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
702	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
703	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
704	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
705	  \[
706	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
707	  \]
708	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
709	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
710	  \[
711	    (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
712	  \]
713	  i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by the
714	  surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
715	\end{lemma}
716	
717	\begin{proof}
718	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
719	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
720	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
721	    \leanok
722	  This reads off the lowest-degree segment of the very long exact sequence of
723	  right-derived functors built, from the same short exact sequence
724	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\), in the proof of
725	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}; here we use only its initial terms, so we
726	  recall the construction briefly and then specialize. By the horseshoe lift
727	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
728	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a degreewise-split short exact
729	  sequence of cochain complexes \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\). Applying the
730	  additive functor \(G\) degreewise preserves each split degree, giving a short exact
731	  sequence of complexes \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in
732	  \(\mathcal{B}\); its complex-level homology long exact sequence
733	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}), transported across the
734	  injective-resolution computation \(H^k(G(I_\bullet)) \cong (R^k G)(-)\)
735	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}) and using
736	  \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}), has low-degree
737	  initial segment
738	  \[
739	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J).
740	  \]
741	  Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), the right
742	  end \((R^1 G)(J)\) vanishes. Hence the segment
743	  \(G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to 0\) is exact with vanishing
744	  right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is the image of
745	  \(G(J) \to G(Z)\). Therefore \(\delta^0\) factors through an isomorphism
746	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \cong\ } (R^1 G)(A)\),
747	  which is the assertion.
748	\end{proof}

750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
801	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
802	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
803	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
804	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
805	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
806	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
807	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
808	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
809	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
810	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
811	\end{proof}
812	
813	\begin{lemma}[Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object of the applied complex]
814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
815	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_hom_iCycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_toCycles}
816	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
817	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
818	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D) — the left-exact initial segment
819	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C)$ specialized to the cosyzygy sequences
820	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
821	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
822	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
823	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
824	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
826	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
829	  \[
830	    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
831	  \]
832	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
833	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
834	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
839	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
840	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
841	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
842	  \[
843	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
844	  \]
845	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
846	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
847	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
848	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
849	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
850	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
851	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
852	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
853	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
854	  kernels under precomposition, so
855	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
856	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
857	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
858	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
859	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
860	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
862	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
863	\end{proof}
864	
865	\begin{lemma}[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
866	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
867	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso, CategoryTheory.Functor.gHomologyZeroIso}
868	  % NOTE (iter-007 review): this lemma states BOTH the degree-0 case (H⁰ ≅ G(A)) and the
869	  % positive-degree case (Hⁿ ≅ coker). The Lean split is unavoidable — the n=0 and n≥1 targets
870	  % have different types — so the \lean{} names both decls: cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso (n≥1)
871	  % and gHomologyZeroIso (n=0). Both are sorry-free + axiom-clean.
872	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
873	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
874	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
875	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
876	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
878	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
879	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
880	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
881	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
894	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).
895	\end{lemma}
896	
897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}

993	% to an exact sequence
994	% $$
995	% 0 \to F(J^n) \to F(I^{n + 1}) \to F(J^{n + 1}) \to 0
996	% $$
997	% This in turn proves that $F(I^\bullet)$ is exact."
998	\begin{proof}
999	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution,
1000	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker,
1001	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
1002	    \leanok
1003	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, now assembled from the cosyzygy
1004	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1005	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1006	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
1007	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.
1008	
1009	  \emph{Degree zero.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution},
1010	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
1011	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
1012	  This is the case \(n = 0\).
1013	
1014	  \emph{Positive degrees.} Fix \(n \geq 1\). The sequences
1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
1018	  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1023	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1024	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})
1025	  \]
1026	  reduces \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor. (For \(n = 1\) the
1027	  staircase is empty and \((R^1 G)(A) = (R^1 G)(Z^0)\).) Now apply the lowest-degree
1028	  cokernel description (Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}) to \((S_{n-1})\),
1029	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), whose middle term \(J^{n-1}\) is
1030	  acyclic:
1031	  \[
1032	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
1033	  \]
1034	  Finally Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution} identifies this cokernel
1035	  with the cohomology of the applied complex,
1036	  \(\operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\). Composing
1037	  the staircase, the cokernel description, and this identification gives
1038	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for every \(n \geq 1\); together with the
1039	  degree-zero case this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
1040	
1041	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the dimension-shift
1042	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} and its lowest-degree companion
1043	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
1044	  horseshoe lift and the complex-level homology long exact sequence on which those
1045	  lemmas rest --- on the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\). The injective
1046	  objects supplied by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
1047	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
1048	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
1049	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
1050	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
1051	\end{proof}
1052

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, definition of $R^i f_*$ as the
5	% $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex
6	% and references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter records the derived-functor higher direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\)
11	for \(i \geq 1\). It provides the single object that the {\v C}ech computation of
12	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} compares against: the comparison
13	theorem \(\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}\) identifies the cohomology of the
14	relative {\v C}ech complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) wherever the derived functor
15	is defined.
16	
17	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category).
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
19	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	
21	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
22	
23	\begin{definition}\leanok
24	[Higher direct image]
25	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
26	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
27	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
28	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
29	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
30	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
31	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
32	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
33	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
34	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
35	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
36	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
37	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
38	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
39	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
40	  % the sheaf
41	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
42	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
43	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
44	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
45	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
46	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
47	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
48	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
49	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
50	  sheafification of the presheaf
51	  \[
52	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
53	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
54	  \]
55	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
56	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
57	\end{definition}

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechAcyclic.lean
5	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/PresheafCech.lean
6	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FreePresheafComplex.lean
7	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechBridge.lean
8	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImagePresheaf.lean
9	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AbsoluteCohomology.lean
10	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechToCohomology.lean
11	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AffineSerreVanishing.lean
12	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/QcohTildeSections.lean
13	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/QcohRestrictBasicOpen.lean
14	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
15	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
16	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
17	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
18	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
19	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
20	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
21	
22	\section{Setup and motivation}
23	
24	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
25	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
26	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
27	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
28	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
29	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
30	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
31	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
32	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
33	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
34	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
35	
36	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
37	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
38	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
39	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
40	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
41	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
42	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
43	\[
44	  \begin{array}{ccc}
45	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
46	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
47	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
48	  \end{array}
49	\]
50	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
51	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
52	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
53	
54	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
55	
56	\begin{definition}
57	
58	\leanok
59	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
60	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
62	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
63	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
64	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
65	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
66	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
67	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
68	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
69	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
70	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
71	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
72	  % the unit ideal, see
73	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
74	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
75	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
76	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
77	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
78	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
79	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
80	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
81	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
82	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
83	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
84	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
85	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
86	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
87	  \[
88	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
89	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
90	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
91	  \]
92	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
93	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
94	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
95	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
96	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
97	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
98	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
99	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
100	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
101	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
102	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
103	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
104	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
105	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
106	\end{definition}
107	
108	\begin{definition}
109	
110	\leanok
111	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
112	  \label{def:cech_complex}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
114	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
115	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
116	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
117	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
118	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
119	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
120	  % is identified with the complex
121	  % $$
122	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
123	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
124	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
125	  % \ldots
126	  % $$
127	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
128	  % \begin{equation}
129	  % \label{equation-extended}
130	  % 0 \to
131	  % M \to
132	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
133	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
134	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
135	  % \ldots
136	  % \end{equation}
137	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
138	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
139	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
140	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
141	  vanishing).}
142	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
143	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
144	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
145	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
146	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
147	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
148	  in degree \(p\) it is
149	  \[
150	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
151	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
152	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
153	  \]
154	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
155	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
156	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
157	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
158	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
159	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
160	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
161	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
162	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
163	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
164	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
165	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
166	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
167	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
168	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
169	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
170	\end{definition}
171	
172	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
173	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
174	
175	It is convenient to factor the construction of
176	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
177	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
178	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
179	
180	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
181	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
182	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
183	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
184	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
185	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
186	\[
187	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
188	    \;=\;
189	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
190	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
191	\]
192	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
193	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
194	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
195	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
196	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
197	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
198	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
199	
200	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}

200	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
201	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
202	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
203	\[
204	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
205	  \qquad
206	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
207	\]
208	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
209	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
210	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
211	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
212	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
213	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
214	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
215	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
216	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
217	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
218	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
219	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
220	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
221	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
222	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
223	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
224	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
225	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
226	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
227	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
228	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
229	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
230	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
231	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
232	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
233	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
234	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
235	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
236	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
237	
238	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
239	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
240	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
241	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
242	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
243	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
244	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
245	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
246	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
247	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
248	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
249	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
250	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
251	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
252	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
253	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
254	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
255	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
256	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
257	
258	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
259	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
260	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
261	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
262	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
263	
264	\begin{definition}\leanok
265	[Arrow of an affine cover]
266	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
268	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
269	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
270	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
271	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
272	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
273	  apply.
274	\end{definition}
275	
276	\begin{definition}[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
277	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
278	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOver, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOverAug}
279	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
280	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
281	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
282	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
283	  \[
284	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
285	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
286	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
287	  \]
288	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
289	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
290	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
291	\end{definition}
292	
293	\begin{definition}\leanok
294	[Push--pull object map]
295	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
296	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
297	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
298	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
299	  \[
300	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
301	  \]
302	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
303	\end{definition}
304	
305	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
306	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
308	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
309	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
310	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
311	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
312	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
313	  \[
314	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
315	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
316	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
318	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
319	      \;=\;
320	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
321	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
322	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
323	  \]
324	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
325	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
326	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
327	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
328	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
329	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
330	\end{definition}

340	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
341	\end{lemma}

4003	  \(U_i\) of the site that covers the terminal object, together with, for each \(i\), a presentation of
4004	  the over-picture restriction \(\mathcal{F}.\operatorname{over} U_i\). A sheaf of modules is
4005	  quasi-coherent precisely when such a datum exists.
4006	\end{lemma}

4008	\begin{lemma}[Sections of a restricted module are sections over the image open]
4009	  \label{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}
4010	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrict_obj}
4011	  \mathlibok
4012	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
4013	  For an \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), an open immersion \(\iota : X \to Y\), and an open
4014	  \(U \subseteq X\), the sections of the restricted module over \(U\) coincide definitionally with the
4015	  sections of \(\mathcal{M}\) over the image open \(\iota(U)\):
4016	  \(\Gamma(\mathcal{M}.\operatorname{restrict} \iota, U) = \Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \iota(U))\), and
4017	  likewise for the restriction maps. The section comparison is therefore an equality, not merely an
4018	  isomorphism.
4019	\end{lemma}

4155	    lem:restrict_obj_mathlib, lem:overEquivalence_isContinuous}
4156	  Both objects describe ``\(\mathcal{F}\) restricted to \(D(g)\)'', but in two different pictures: the
4157	  over-picture indexes the opens of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) lying under \(D(g)\), whereas
4158	  \(\operatorname{modulesRestrictBasicOpen} g\, \mathcal{F}\) is a sheaf on the affine scheme
4159	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R_g\) via the comparison \(D(g) \cong \operatorname{Spec} R_g\)
4160	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen}). The bridge is assembled in three sub-steps.
4161	
4162	  \emph{Step B3a (the structure-sheaf compatibility datum).} The site equivalence underlying the bridge
4163	  is the over-site equivalence
4164	  \((\operatorname{Opens} \operatorname{Spec} R).\operatorname{over} D(g) \simeq
4165	  \operatorname{Opens}(\widehat{D(g)})\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:overEquivalence_isContinuous} (now known to be
4166	  continuous), between the over-site and the subspace site of the open
4167	  \(\widehat{D(g)} \subseteq \operatorname{Spec} R\). Across this equivalence the two structure sheaves
4168	  are genuinely different presentations of \(\mathcal{O}\): on the over-side it is
4169	  \(\mathcal{S} = (\operatorname{Spec} R).\operatorname{ringCatSheaf}.\operatorname{over} D(g)\), whose
4170	  sections over an over-object \(V\) are \(\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, V)\); on the subspace side it is
4171	  the subscheme structure sheaf \((\widehat{D(g)}).\operatorname{ringCatSheaf}\), whose sections over an
4172	  open \(W \subseteq \widehat{D(g)}\) are \(\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \iota(W))\) for the image open
4173	  along the inclusion. These agree on sections because \(\iota\) is an open immersion, and the
4174	  comparison ring-sheaf morphisms \(\varphi, \psi\) (with their coherence witnesses \(H_1, H_2\)) are
4175	  built from the open-immersion structure-sheaf isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom.appIso}\) of the
4176	  inclusion \((\operatorname{specBasicOpen} g).\iota\) — the \emph{same} \(\operatorname{appIso}\) that
4177	  \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor}\) uses to build its ring map. This is genuine
4178	  geometric content: unlike step B2, where both ring sheaves were \(\operatorname{over}\) of the same
4179	  base and related by the identity ring homomorphism, here the two ring sheaves live on
4180	  different sites and the comparison is a non-trivial open-immersion isomorphism.
4181	
4182	  \emph{Step B3b (the module equivalence on the subspace).} Feed the comparison data
4183	  \(\varphi, \psi, H_1, H_2\) and the now-continuous site equivalence
4184	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:overEquivalence_isContinuous}) into
4185	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}, obtaining an equivalence of categories of
4186	  sheaves of modules
4187	  \((\widehat{D(g)}).\mathrm{Modules} \simeq \operatorname{SheafOfModules}
4188	  \bigl((\operatorname{Spec} R).\operatorname{ringCatSheaf}.\operatorname{over} D(g)\bigr)\)
4189	  whose object map sends the subscheme restriction
4190	  \(\mathcal{F}.\operatorname{restrict} (\operatorname{specBasicOpen} g).\iota\) to the over-picture
4191	  object \(\mathcal{F}.\operatorname{over} D(g)\); on sections the two agree by the definitional equality
4192	  of Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}.
4193	
4194	  \emph{Step B3c (transport to \(\operatorname{Spec} R_g\) and assemble).} Transport from the subspace
4195	  \(\widehat{D(g)}\) to the affine \(\operatorname{Spec} R_g\) along the isomorphism
4196	  \(\operatorname{basicOpenIsoSpecAway} g\): restriction along an isomorphism is an in-framework
4197	  equivalence of \(\mathrm{Modules}\)-categories, and it lands on
4198	  \(\operatorname{modulesRestrictBasicOpen} g\, \mathcal{F}\), which is definitionally the iterated
4199	  (double) restriction (Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen}). Composing the object isomorphism of
4200	  step B3b with this transport yields the required bridge
4201	  \(\mathcal{F}.\operatorname{over} D(g) \cong \operatorname{modulesRestrictBasicOpen} g\, \mathcal{F}\).
4202	
4203	  This obligation is genuinely distinct from the structure-sheaf base change
4204	  \(\widetilde{M}|_{D(f)} = \widetilde{M_f}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:tilde_restrict_basicOpen}) and is
4205	  \emph{not} discharged by the comparison isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen},
4206	  which relates two scheme-picture restrictions and never mentions the over-picture.
4207	\end{proof}

3827	\section{Route B: the 01I8 counit isomorphism via section-localization}
3828	
3829	The unconditional 01I8 instance \([{\,}\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\,\mathcal{F}{\,}] \Rightarrow
3830	\operatorname{IsIso}(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma})\) on \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) is established here
3831	along the \emph{section-localization} route (Route B), the shortest path aligned with the ambient
3832	library's \(\widetilde{(-)} / \operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma}\) layer. That layer states the entire
3833	local theory of \(\widetilde{(-)}\) in the language of \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\): the
3834	structure-sheaf-localization map \(\widetilde{(-)}.\operatorname{toOpen}\) exhibits
3835	\(\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde{M}) = M_f\) as a localization at the powers of \(f\), and the
3836	distinguished-open component of the counit \(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma}\) is \emph{literally} the
3837	localization lift \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.lift}\) of the section-restriction map along that
3838	identification. Route B therefore reduces 01I8 to a single keystone — that the section-restriction
3839	map \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) of a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
3840	localization at the powers of \(f\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule}) — and then checks
3841	the counit on the basis of distinguished opens. Compared with the alternative global-presentation
3842	route (Route A), Route B replaces that route's two genuine-mathematics obligations — the

4612	  Let \(R\) be a ring, \(M\) an \(R\)-module, and \(f \in R\). Under the identification
4613	  \(D(f) \cong \operatorname{Spec} R_f\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen}, the
4614	  restriction of the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) to the standard open \(D(f)\) is
4615	  isomorphic to the associated sheaf of the away-localised module:
4616	  \(\widetilde{M}\,|_{D(f)} \cong \widetilde{M_f}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
4617	  R_f}\)-modules, where \(M_f = (\operatorname{powers} f)^{-1} M\).
4618	  \emph{Route-A fallback (tilde base-change), off the live 01I8 path; see
4619	  \texttt{analogies/o1i8-route.md}.}
4620	\end{lemma}
4621	\begin{proof}
4622	  \uses{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen}
4623	  Transport \(\widetilde{M}\,|_{D(f)}\) to an \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
4624	  R_f}\)-module by Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen}. On a standard open
4625	  \(D(g) \subseteq D(f)\) the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\,|_{D(f)}\) are the localisation
4626	  \(M_{gf}\), which is exactly the localisation of \(M_f\) at (the image of) \(g\); since
4627	  \(\psi^{-1}(D(f)) = D(\psi^\sharp(f))\) for the localisation map \(\psi^\sharp : R \to R_f\),
4628	  the value on \(D(f)\) itself is \(M_f\). These identifications are compatible with
4629	  restriction, hence patch to an isomorphism of sheaves
4630	  \(\widetilde{M}\,|_{D(f)} \cong \widetilde{M_f}\) over \(\operatorname{Spec} R_f\).
4631	\end{proof}
4632	
4633	\begin{lemma}[Presentations restrict along an open immersion of basic opens]
4634	  \label{lem:presentation_restrict_basicOpen}
4635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.presentation_restrict_basicOpen}
4636	  \uses{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen, lem:tilde_restrict_basicOpen}
4637	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine} (proof),

4639	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "for every prime $\mathfrak p \subset R$ corresponding to $x \in X$
4640	  % there exists an open neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$ such that
4641	  % $\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to the quasi-coherent
4642	  % sheaf associated to some $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module $M$.
4643	  % In other words, we get an open covering by $U$'s with this property.
4644	  % By Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} for example we can refine this
4645	  % covering to  a standard open covering."
4646	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine} (proof),
4647	  Tag 01I8.}
4648	  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\), let \(\mathcal{F}\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, let
4649	  \(U \subseteq X\) be open, and suppose \(\mathcal{F}|_U\) admits a presentation
4650	  \(\mathcal{O}_U^{(J)} \to \mathcal{O}_U^{(I)} \to \mathcal{F}|_U \to 0\). Then for every
4651	  basic open \(D(g) \subseteq U\), the restricted module
4652	  \(\mathcal{F}|_{D(g)}\) — viewed as an \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R_g}\)-module via
4653	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen} — admits a presentation
4654	  \(\mathcal{O}^{(J)} \to \mathcal{O}^{(I)} \to \mathcal{F}|_{D(g)} \to 0\) over
4655	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R_g\); in particular \(\mathcal{F}|_{D(g)}\) is quasi-coherent over
4656	  \(R_g\).
4657	  \emph{Route-A fallback (P1a base-change chain), off the live 01I8 path; see
4658	  \texttt{analogies/o1i8-route.md}.}
4659	\end{lemma}
4660	\begin{proof}
4661	  \uses{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen, lem:tilde_restrict_basicOpen}
4662	  Restriction of sheaves of modules along the open immersion
4663	  \(D(g) \hookrightarrow U\) is an additive functor that preserves the structure sheaf, hence
4664	  preserves cokernels and arbitrary direct sums of \(\mathcal{O}\); applying it to the given
4665	  right-exact sequence yields a right-exact sequence
4666	  \(\mathcal{O}_{D(g)}^{(J)} \to \mathcal{O}_{D(g)}^{(I)} \to \mathcal{F}|_{D(g)} \to 0\).
4667	  Transporting along \(D(g) \cong \operatorname{Spec} R_g\)
4668	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen}) turns the free modules
4669	  \(\mathcal{O}_{D(g)}^{(I)}\), \(\mathcal{O}_{D(g)}^{(J)}\) into the free
4670	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R_g}\)-modules \(\widetilde{R_g}^{(I)}\),
4671	  \(\widetilde{R_g}^{(J)}\) (the structure sheaf restricts to the structure sheaf, and free
4672	  modules restrict to free modules by Lemma~\ref{lem:tilde_restrict_basicOpen}). This is a
4673	  presentation of \(\mathcal{F}|_{D(g)}\) over \(\operatorname{Spec} R_g\), exhibiting it as

4695	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let \(f \in R\). Then, transporting along the canonical
4696	  isomorphism of affine schemes \(D(f) \cong \operatorname{Spec} R_f\)
4697	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen}), the restriction \(\mathcal{F}|_{D(f)}\) is a
4698	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R_f}\)-module, and in particular it admits a

content:
# Strategy

## Goal

Prove `AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage` (`lem:cech_computes_cohomology`),
the protected, frozen-signature target: for `f : X ⟶ S` separated and quasi-compact, `F`
quasi-coherent, `𝒰` a finite affine open cover of `X`, an isomorphism in the weak existence
form `Nonempty ((CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i ≅ higherDirectImage f i F)` (under
`[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`), with `higherDirectImage f i F =
((pushforward f).rightDerived i).obj F`. End-state: zero inline `sorry` in the cone, zero
project axioms, kernel-only axioms.

## Phases & estimations

| Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC | Key Mathlib needs | Risks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 02KG `affine_serre_vanishing` (cover-system + affine instantiation) | ACTIVE | ~1 | ~40–120 | Gated top theorems `affine_cech_vanishing_qcoh`/`affine_serre_vanishing` consume unconditional `qcoh_iso_tilde_sections`. | Cover-system chain COMPLETE; two tops FALSE-ready in the graph — do NOT dispatch until 01I8 closes (see Routes). |
| 01I8 `F≅~(ΓF)` via section-localization (Route B) | ACTIVE | ~2–4 | ~200–400 | Keystone `IsLocalizedModule (.powers f) (Γ(X,F)→Γ(D(f),F))` for qcoh `F` via B1–B6 chain; load-bearing lane B3 `restrict-over-compat` (engine `pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence`). | One bounded categorical bridge B3 (over↔scheme picture); `IsLocalizing`/`isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizing` DO NOT exist (analogist `bridge`); right-exactness pitfall (see Routes). |
| P5a vanishing inputs (consume P3b) | ACTIVE | ~3–4 | ~200–400 | `Hⁿ(f⁻¹V,G)` Ext-bridge + open-immersion/affine vanishing + augmented-Čech resolution (01XJ leaf done). | Ext-by-injective-resolution-of-2nd-arg backing present (`InjectiveResolution.extEquivCohomologyClass`). |
| P5b comparison assembly | BLOCKED | ~2–3 | ~150–300 | P4 engine + P5a resolution + termwise `f_*`-acyclicity. | Final Route-A assembly of the protected goal. |

## Completed

| Phase | Iters (done@ · used) | LOC | Files | Key results | Reusable techniques | Pitfalls |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 push–pull functor laws | 002 · 2 | ~120 | `CechHigherDirectImage.lean` | `pushPullMap_comp` closing push–pull functoriality | object-form align `simp [Functor.comp_obj]` before `reassoc_of%`; `rawPushPullMap`+`subst`+pentagon | `conjugateEquiv_comp` mate route INFEASIBLE (kernel `whnf` blow-up) |
| P2 `CechNerve`/`CechComplex` | 002 · 1 | ~60 | `CechHigherDirectImage.lean` | `pushPullFunctor`, `coverCechNerve(Aug)`, `CechNerve`, `CechComplex` axiom-clean | `Over.lift`+`.rightOp`+`CosimplicialObject.Augmented.whiskeringObj`; terminal augmentation | none |
| P4 acyclic-resolution lemma (Leray, 015E) | 009 · 6 | ~965 | `AcyclicResolution.lean` | `rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution`, `rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic`, horseshoe `ofShortExact`, dimension shift — axiom-clean | decompose-then-build; two-step `cokernel.mapIso`; `Nat.rec` staircase off `stairGen` | `ShortComplex.mapCyclesIso` WRONG for left-exact functor; `← G.map_comp` silently fails by a mapped-complex term |
| P3 standard-cover Čech vanishing (section form, tilde) | 022 · ~14 | ~1200 | `CechAcyclic.lean` | `sectionCech_affine_vanishing` + `sectionCech_homology_exact` (IsZero homology p≥1) for `F=~M`; L1 tilde-bridge + L3 combinatorial core | accessors DEFEQ (`rfl`); abstract section maps (`set;clear_value`) before `IsLocalizedModule.ext`; target ABSOLUTE section complex | residual qcoh `F≅~ΓF` (01I8) deferred to 02KG consumer; old relative `affine` sorry superseded |
| P3b Čech↔derived bridge bricks | 025 · ~9 | ~1500 | `PresheafCech`,`FreePresheafComplex`,`CechBridge` | `cechFreeComplex_quasiIso` (free resolution of `O_𝒰`), `ses_cech_h1`, `injective_cech_acyclic` (pos-degree Čech vanishing for injectives) — axiom-clean | op-transport via `opFunctor`+`preadditiveYoneda`; `erw`+`.trans` for defeq-carrier mismatch; Route-B arrow-iso transfer | `maxHeartbeats 2000000` needed; LSP-staleness (trust only `lake env lean`) |
| Absolute-cohomology Form B + 01EO general basis criterion | 028 · 3 | ~600 | `AbsoluteCohomology`,`CechToCohomology` | `jShriekOU`=sheafify(free(yoneda U)); H⁰≅Γ (+naturality); 01EO chain L1–L4+top (`cech_eq_cohomology_of_basis`); `BasisCovSystem`/`HasVanishingHigherCech` cover-system encoding | Form B kills restriction-preserves-injectives (injective in 2nd Ext arg); cover-local presheaf form; `attribute [local instance] hasExtModules`; AB4* `Epi(Pi.map)` elementwise | `[EnoughInjectives X.Modules]` absent in Mathlib → carried as explicit hyp (P5a convention); 3 first-attempt iters, no churn |

## Routes

### Route A — acyclic-resolution comparison (CHOSEN)
The augmented Čech complex `0 → F → C⁰ → C¹ → ⋯` on `X` (`Cᵖ = ∏ (j_s)_*(F|_{U_s})`) is
(i) a resolution of `F` and (ii) termwise `(pushforward f)`-acyclic. The P4 abstract lemma
gives `Hⁱ(f_* C•) ≅ Rⁱf_* F` directly — ONE abstract lemma, NO spectral sequences. Acyclicity
input (ii) reduces to affine Serre vanishing `H^q(affine, qcoh)=0`, supplied by the P3b bridge.

### Route SS — two spectral sequences (REJECTED)
Both absent from Mathlib (multi-thousand-LOC); rests on the same `injective_cech_acyclic` brick as A.
(Named "Route SS" to avoid collision with the 01I8-internal "Route B" section-localization route below.)

### The acyclicity bridge (torsor-free, load-bearing)
Route A's acyclicity reduces to affine Serre vanishing `H^q(Spec A, qcoh)=0` (02KG). The honest
non-circular route is the minimal Čech↔derived bridge: (1) `injective_cech_acyclic`, (2)
`ses_cech_h1`, (3) the dimension-shift `cech_eq_cohomology_of_basis` (01EO) consuming the
standard-cover Čech vanishing of P3 as its condition (3). All three bricks of (1)+(2) are done;
01EO is the next target. This breaks the circular regress: P3 produces standard-cover Čech
vanishing; the bridge lifts it to affine sheaf vanishing without ever using affine vanishing.

### 01I8 affine `F≅~(ΓF)` — section-localization (Route B; the live route)
Selected over the global-presentation route (Route P) because P only reached the goal through TWO
genuine-mathematics walls (tilde base-change `tilde_restrict_basicOpen` + `tildePreservesFiniteLimits`
+ qcoh-closed-under-kernels). Route B converts those two walls into ONE bounded categorical bridge
(B3 below); it is strictly better, but it is NOT base-change-free (analogist `bridge`). Keystone — for
qcoh `F`, `IsLocalizedModule (.powers f)
(Γ(X,F)→Γ(D(f),F))` — built as the explicit B1–B6 chain (NO `IsLocalizing` shortcut: `IsLocalizing`
and `isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizing` DO NOT exist in Mathlib — Tilde's `IsQuasicoherent` section
ends at `isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation`):
- **B0 [DONE]** `exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover`, `isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover` (P1b),
  `section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation` + 2 local-model bricks, `modulesRestrictBasicOpen`(+`Iso`).
- **B1 [small]** `QuasicoherentData F` → cover `Uᵢ` + per-`Uᵢ` `Presentation(F.over Uᵢ)`; refine to finite
  `D(gⱼ)⊆U_{φⱼ}`, `span{gⱼ}=R` via B0 subcover.
- **B2 [medium]** restrict `Presentation(F.over U_{φⱼ})` → `Presentation(F.over D(gⱼ))` via
  `Presentation.map` (over is left-adjoint + unit-iso). Pure `over`-picture, independent of B3.
- **B3 [THE BRIDGE — load-bearing lane]** `F.over D(gⱼ) ≅ modulesRestrictBasicOpen gⱼ F` (over-picture ↔
  honest `(Spec R_{gⱼ}).Modules`), engine `pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence`. Distinct from
  `lemma-widetilde-pullback`; NOT discharged by `modulesRestrictBasicOpenIso`.
- **B4 [small]** transport presentation across B3 + `modulesRestrictBasicOpenIso` (`Presentation.ofIsIso`)
  → `Presentation(modulesRestrictBasicOpen gⱼ F)`.
- **B5/B6 [DONE consumers]** `section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation` per `gⱼ`; descend with
  `isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover` (section comparison is `restrict_obj`-`rfl`).
Then assembly: each `D(f)`-component of `fromTildeΓ` IS `IsLocalizedModule.lift` of the keystone map ⟹
`IsIso fromTildeΓ` ⟹ unconditional `qcoh_iso_tilde_sections`. **Non-circularity pitfall (load-bearing):**
the per-`D(gⱼ)` identification `F|_{D(gⱼ)}≅~Mⱼ` MUST come from tilde RIGHT-exactness (left adjoint
preserves the cokernel of the presentation), NOT a left-exact `Γ(D(gⱼ),-)` of an abstract cokernel —
the latter silently needs affine `H¹`-vanishing and reopens the 02KG circularity. `modulesRestrictBasicOpen`
(in `QcohRestrictBasicOpen`) is REUSED by B3/B4 — that file is NOT dormant. `TildeExactness` stays
dormant. Milestones: B1+B2 (over-picture) ∥ B3 (bridge) → B4 → keystone → assembly → unconditional
`qcoh_iso_tilde_sections`.

### Absolute cohomology realization — Ext of the corepresenting object (Form B)
`H^p(U, F) := Ext^p_{X.Modules}(jShriekOU U, F)` where `jShriekOU U := sheafify(free(yoneda U))`
is the corepresenting object of `F ↦ Γ(U,F) = F(U)` (`= j_!O_U` up to iso), built from the
already-shipped `freeYonedaHomEquiv` + `sheafificationAdjunction` (~50–80 LOC). The three facts
01EO consumes are then off-the-shelf with the SES staying in `X.Modules`: injective vanishing
`Ext.eq_zero_of_injective` (injective `I` as the 2nd arg — no restriction taken), covariant LES
`Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁/₂/₃` at fixed 1st arg `jShriekOU U`, and `H⁰ ≅ Γ(U,F)` via the
corepresentability chain. The discarded alternatives: Form A `Ext(O_U, F|_U)` needs
restriction-preserves-injectives (≈ a 200–500 LOC `j_!` functor); `Functor.rightDerived` has
free injective vanishing but no packaged LES; `Sheaf.H` is absent. Reversal signal: Ext
universe/smallness pain over `SheafOfModules` → fall back to Route γ (Čech colimit), never `Sheaf.H`.

## Open strategic questions

- **EnoughInjectives connector (cone obligation).** The frozen target provides
  `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]` but the 01EO/02KG cone carries the stronger
  `[EnoughInjectives X.Modules]`. Connector `HasInjectiveResolutions C → EnoughInjectives C` is TRUE,
  ~6 LOC (deg-0 mono into `I.cocomplex.X 0` via `CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.instMonoFNatι`).
  Build + register at the P5b assembly lane — DISSOLVES the "EnoughInjectives absent in Mathlib" worry.
- P5a last-mile bridge `Hᵏ((f_*I^•)(V)) = Ext^k(jShriek(f⁻¹V), G)`: Ext-by-injective-resolution-of-2nd-arg
  backing CONFIRMED present (`InjectiveResolution.extEquivCohomologyClass`). Due when P5a consumers dispatch.
- 02KG `surj_of_vanishing` route: `ses_cech_h1` + `O_X`-epi local section surjectivity via
  `Presheaf.IsLocallySurjective`, gated on the `toSheaf` epi-preservation BUILD (= `PreservesFiniteColimits
  (SheafOfModules.toSheaf)`, route confirmed, `analogies/tosheaf-epi.md`); refine the lift cover to affine
  opens (`standard_cover_cofinal`, done) to land a standard cover in `Cov`.
- 01I8 section-localization route detail in Routes (`### 01I8 affine F≅~(ΓF)`). The `IsLocalizing`
  shortcut is OFF the table (analogist `bridge`: neither `IsLocalizing` nor `isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizing`
  exists). Assembly uses the explicit per-`D(f)`-component `IsLocalizedModule.lift` identity (Mathlib
  `Tilde.fromTildeΓ`) + `isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff` (essImage form) — confirm the iso-reflection at assembly.

## Mathlib gaps & new material

Gaps to fill (project-side, Mathlib lacks):
- Standard-cover Čech complex exactness `0→M→∏M_{f_i}→⋯` (P3) — done via L1+L3 around `exact_of_isLocalized_span`.
- Presheaf-level Čech machinery for `O_X`-modules (P3b free/section complexes, `injective_cech_acyclic`,
  `ses_cech_h1`) — done. Dropped as off-critical-path: presheaf enough-injectives + δ-functor universality.
- Absolute module-valued `Hⁿ(U,F)` = `Ext^p(jShriekOU U, F)` via `Abelian.Ext` (Form B); corepresenting
  object `jShriekOU = sheafify(free(yoneda U))`. NOT a bespoke `j_!` functor (that is absent, 200–500 LOC).
- Relative/open-immersion affine vanishing `R^q(jₛ)_*=0`, `R^i(affine)_*=0` (P5).
- EnoughInjectives connector `HasInjectiveResolutions C → EnoughInjectives C` (~6 LOC, instance) — see
  Open questions; makes the 01EO/02KG cone type-check against the frozen target's weaker hypothesis.
- `PreservesFiniteColimits (SheafOfModules.toSheaf)` — the missing dual of Mathlib's `PreservesFiniteLimits`
  (toSheaf right-exactness), NOT a small instance: ~80–150 LOC via the sheafification square + left-adjoint
  reflector, never through `forget` (`analogies/tosheaf-epi.md`). `toSheaf` epi-preservation is then a
  corollary, unlocking `Presheaf.IsLocallySurjective` for `surj_of_vanishing`.
- 01I8 instance `[IsQuasicoherent F] → IsIso F.fromTildeΓ` via Route B: remaining gap is the B1–B6
  keystone chain, whose single load-bearing build is B3 `restrict-over-compat`
  (`F.over D(gⱼ) ≅ modulesRestrictBasicOpen gⱼ F`) via `pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence` at the
  open-subscheme site equivalence. NO `tildePreservesFiniteLimits`, NO `tilde_restrict_basicOpen`,
  NO `IsLocalizing`. ~150–350 LOC, fiddly site/IsContinuous plumbing, no math obstruction.
  (`analogies/bridge.md`.)

New project material:
- push–pull functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`, `p ↦ p_* p^* F` — hand-rolled.
- `CechNerve`/`CechComplex`; `AcyclicResolution.lean` (P4); P3b free-presheaf + section Čech complexes,
  `cechFreeComplex_quasiIso`, `injective_cech_acyclic`, `ses_cech_h1`.
- `AbsoluteCohomology.lean`: `jShriekOU`, corepresentability iso, `H^p := Ext^p(jShriekOU U, -)`, H⁰=Γ,
  injective-vanishing + LES wrappers → 01EO `cech_eq_cohomology_of_basis` → 02KG `affine_serre_vanishing`.
- comparison assembly `cech_computes_higherDirectImage` via Route A.

Good. Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me now also look at the remaining STRATEGY section to check all phases, and then look at the sections I haven't audited yet in the `Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex` chapter. 

Now let me do a rapid scan of the key pending sections of `Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex`, specifically:
1. Check if there are any `\needs \lean{}` nodes (without lean{})
2. Check the section on tilePreservesKernels etc.
3. Check the final assembly section
I'll run leandag to identify any blueprint nodes that are missing their Lean implementations.

2259	  contracting homotopy is built at the engine-complex level as
2260	  \(\operatorname{cechEnginePrepend}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex}; its
2261	  evaluated-complex avatar is obtained by transporting along the degreewise iso
2262	  \(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalEngineIso}\) of

2264	  \emph{prepend homotopy} is the family of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-linear maps
2265	  \[
2266	    h_p : K(\mathcal{U})_p(V) \longrightarrow K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1}(V),
2267	    \qquad

2272	      \end{cases}
2273	  \]
2274	  defined on the \(I_1\)-indexed direct sum of
2275	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise} as the \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}\) of
2276	  the per-summand prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) maps. This is exactly the
2277	  combinatorial content of \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy}\) of
2278	  Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}, here with the single
2279	  coefficient module \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), transported across the degreewise
2280	  identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_engine_iso}. The maps \(h_p\) (together with the
2281	  degree \((-1)\!\to\!0\) map onto \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\))
2282	  are the homotopy hom maps assembled into a
